title: W05.Deck.fins3646
body:
FINS3646: Toolkit for Finance Week 5: Lecture

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Overview

Modules and Packages  Creating a Module  Accessing functionality from other modules  Including test cases and examples using __name__ Docstrings and Comments IO and Filepaths  Filepaths  IO and Filepaths  Best Practice

Modules and packages  Modules and packages

Modules are an Python object that serves as an organisation unit:

A module is any file of Python code.  For example, PyCharm’s default Python project includes a file called main.py. Thus, the code in this file resides in a module called main.  If you were working on a Finance project, you might group code related to stock tickers in a file called ticker.py.

Code in each module is distinct.  Modules have a namespace containing all objects defined in the module  Code in one module is not automatically accessible in another – an import statement is required.  Importing the module provides access to the defined objects.

Modules and packages

Packages are collections of interrelated modules grouped under a shared package namespace.

Modules defined inside a package have their own namespace, which is nested within the package namespace. The presence of a special __init__.py file (an __init__ module) informs Python that the directory is a package. Importing the packages allows us to access the contained modules

While we will use packages in the class, we will not discuss creating a package.

Recall our bank withdrawal code: def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet):

if withdrawal_amount <= account_balance: account_balance = account_balance - withdrawal_amount cash_in_wallet = cash_in_wallet + withdrawal_amount print("Withdrawal successful.") else: print("Insufficient funds. Withdrawal denied.")

return account_balance, cash_in_wallet

account_balance = 500.00 # Current balance in dollars cash_in_wallet = 100.00 # Current cash on hand

account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 50, cash_in_wallet) account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 75, cash_in_wallet) account_balance, cash_in_wallet = withdrawal(account_balance, 125, cash_in_wallet)

print(f"Final account balance: ${account_balance:.2f}") print(f"Final cash in wallet: ${cash_in_wallet:.2f}")

A module is the best way to make sure we can use this function in different files.

Save the function as automated_teller_machine.py

1. Open PyCharm and right-click on the toolkit project folder. 2. Choose New » Python File 3. Name this file automated_teller_machine.py 4. Open this module in PyCharm and copy the function below: def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet):

if withdrawal_amount <= account_balance: account_balance = account_balance - withdrawal_amount cash_in_wallet = cash_in_wallet + withdrawal_amount print("Withdrawal successful.") else: print("Insufficient funds. Withdrawal denied.")

return account_balance, cash_in_wallet

Execute automated_teller_machine.py and nothing will happen:

PyCharm will tell you that the code finished with exit code 0 (meaning, no errors). The module only defines the function. It does not do anything with the function.

We need to import our module before using it:

Start a new interactive Python session by opening the PyCharm console and then type: >>> withdrawal(500, 50, 100) NameError: name 'withdrawal' is not defined

Python raises an exception: The name withdrawal is not available in either the global or built-in namespaces. We need to tell Python to access withdrawal from a module

We access other modules through import statements.

To import an entire module: import <module_name>  Brings the module namespace into scope as <module_name>  Access functions and classes using dot notation: <module_name>.function() import <module_name> as <alias>  Brings the entire module namespace into scope as the <alias>  Access functions and classes using using dot notation under the alias: <alias>.function()

To import specific functions or classes: from <module_name> import item1[, item2]  Brings the item(s) referenced into scope in the current namespace  Access items directly as item1. Dot notation is not required.  Multiple items may be specified with a comma-separated list of names  Using a wildcard * to bring in all items in the module is not recommended. This can lead to name conflicts and unclear code.

We can use our withdrawal function by:

1. Importing the module and using a qualified name:

>>> import automated_teller_machine automated_teller_machine.withdrawal(500, 50, 100)

2. Importing the module with an alias and using a qualified name: >>> import automated_teller_machine as atm atm.withdrawal(500, 50, 100)

3. Importing the name from module and using an unqualified name: >>> from automated_teller_machine import withdrawal withdrawal(500, 50, 100)

Aside: Tips on the Python console

The Python console is useful for testing code. Occasionally, you will need to reset it:

Press the little curved arrow (Rerun) on the left of the console tab. Resetting a session reset the global namespace; all names defined in the previous session (including imported modules) will not be available unless they are re-imported.

We should test every single function or module we define.

There are multiple approaches for testing user-defined functions. The easiest option is to include a small example inside the relevant modules. In a large project, using a testing framework such as PyTest is recommended.

This courses focuses on the simplest solution, placing test code inside modules.

Any test code added directly to a module will be executed whenever the module is imported:

Add the following test code to the end of the automated_teller_machine module: balance = 500.00 # Initial balance in dollars cash = 100.00 # Initial cash on hand

balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 50, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 75, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 125, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 1000, cash)

print(f"Final account balance: ${balance}. Correct value is 250.") print(f"Final cash: ${cash}. Correct value is 350.")

Whenever the module is imported, Python will print the test cash final account balance and cash values.

Python has a __name__ variable that indicates whether a module was executed or imported.

Executed:  When run directly, __name__ = "__main__" inside the module.  e.g., when running automated_teller_machine.py, the value of __name__ inside the module is the string "__main__".

Imported:  When imported: __name__ is set to the module’s name without the extension inside the module  e.g., when importing automated_teller_machine.py, the value of __name__ inside the module is the string "automated_teller_machine".

We can check if __name__ == "__main__" and ensure that the tests are performed only when we execute the module directly.

Change the end of automated_teller_machine.py to: # Example if __name__ == "__main__": balance = 500.00 # Initial balance in dollars cash = 100.00 # Initial cash on hand

balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 50, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 75, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 125, cash) balance, cash = withdrawal(balance, 1000, cash)

print(f"Final account balance: ${balance}. Correct value is 250.") print(f"Final cash: ${cash}. Correct value is 350.")

There are two principle reasons to add descriptive information in your code:

1. Provide high level documentation that tells others (and yourself) what a module or function does. 2. Write comments as reminders of how statements work or the logic behind a complex algorithm.

“Code is more often read than written” – Guido van Rossum (Creator of Python)

Documentation is used to describe the use and functionality of modules, functions, classes, and methods.

Python documentation is usually provided through docstrings Docstrings are surrounded by triple quotes Docstrings are easily available to functions like help. For example: >>> def add(a,b): ... """ Returns the sum of two numbers """ ... return a + b >>> help(add) Help on function add in module __main__: add(a, b) Returns the sum of two numbers

Comments are used to either explain parts of your code that may not be obvious or to keep other notes

A hash symbol # comments out code to the end of the line. Commented code is ignored by the Python. Inside string literals, hash symbols are treated as part of the string (e.g., "a string hash symbol #"), not the beginning of a comment

Modules: Docstring styles

We will use the NumPy docstring style

The NumPy Docstring Style is available from https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html is both simple and widely used. To change the default docstring format in PyCharm and to take advantage of its code-completion functionality: 1. Click on PyCharm » Preferences. . . 2. In the Preferences window, navigate to Tools » Python Integrated Tools. 3. Under Docstrings, set the Docstring Format to “NumPy”

Ed contains a summary of the NumPy Docstring style for your reference.

There are there are other docstring styles, each with its own specific guidelines.

Here’s a NumPy style docstring for our withdrawal function. def withdrawal(account_balance, withdrawal_amount, cash_in_wallet): """ Attempt to withdraw money from a bank account and update wallet cash.

If the `withdrawal_amount` is less than or equal to the `account_balance`, the withdrawal is approved, and the amount is deducted from the account and added to the wallet. Otherwise, the withdrawal is denied.

Parameters account_balance : float Current balance in the bank account. withdrawal_amount : float Amount of money to withdraw. cash_in_wallet : float Current amount of cash in the user's wallet.

Returns account_balance : float Updated bank account balance after the attempted withdrawal. cash_in_wallet : float Updated amount of cash in the wallet after the attempted withdrawal.

Notes A message is printed indicating whether the withdrawal was successful or denied due to insufficient funds. """ <function_code_block>

Input/Output (IO)  Input/Output (IO)

Most code we write for Financial analysis needs to work with information stored in files:

Financial Data: Datasets with stock prices, trading volumes, or economic indicators are commonly stored in CSV or text files. Configuration Files: Placing important parameters and confidential information in code is inadvisable. Configuration files can store things API keys, passwords, or data paths to made code more flexible and secure. Saving Results: Analysis created by our programs need to be saved so they can be shared or analysed further.

While Python permits us to work with a large number of file types, this class will focus on reading information from and writing to text files.

Reading from a file traditionally involves three steps:

file = open(file_path, "r") contents = file.read()

Steps: 1. open(file_path, mode) -> <io.TextIOWrapper>:  Opens the file at the specified file_path location. This is usually a string (str) or a filepath (Path) object.  We indicate to Python that we want to read a file with the mode parameter "r".  Returns an file object, raising an error if there is a problem. In the case of a text file, Python returns a file object of the io.TextIOWrapper class.  In this case, we open a file and assign the io.TextIOWrapper instance to the name file. 2. <io.TextIOWrapper>.read() -> str  Reads all the information from the file object.  In this case of <io.TextIOWrapper>, this returns a str that assigned to the name contents. 3. <io.TextIOWrapper>.close() -> None  Closes the file.  This is an important step. Closing the file also helps prevent data corruption, ensures efficient resource, management and permits other programs to access the data while the Python program continues.

Always close files after use!

There are three methods that read text from a file object:

<io.TextIoWrapper>.read() -> str Read the entire file into a string.

<io.TextIoWrapper>.readlines() -> [str] Read the entire file into a list of strings, one for each line.

<io.TextIoWrapper>.readline() -> str Read a single line from the file. Typically used in while loops.

However, file iteration allows Python to process one line at a time:

filename = 'yourfile.txt' for line in file: <code_block_to_process_line>

File iteration is generally the best approach for reading a file.

Once a file has been read, Python cannot read it again:

filename = 'yourfile.txt' first_read_contents = file.read() second_read_contents = file.read()

second_read_contents will be the empty string, ""

Close and reopen a file that you need to read twice:

filename = 'yourfile.txt' first_read_contents = file.read()

second_read_contents = file.read()

Input/Output (IO): Writing files

Writing to a file traditionally involves three steps:

file = open(file_path, "w") file.write("Hello, world!\n") file.write("Goodbye from Python.\n")

Steps: 1. open(file_path, mode) -> <io.TextIOWrapper>:  Opens the file at the specified file_path location. This is usually a string (str) or a filepath (Path) object.  We indicate to Python that we want to write to a file with the mode parameter: • "w" will overwrite any existing existing file. • "x" will throw an error if the file already exists.  Returns an file object, raising an error if there is a problem. In the case of a text file, Python returns a file object of the io.TextIOWrapper class. 2. <io.TextIOWrapper>.write(str) -> int  Writes <str> to the file object.  Including a newline character "\n" in a string ensures that any subsequent write statement occurs on a new line. Try the code above without the newline characters.  Returns an int indicating the number of characters written to the file. This can be ignored in many cases, such as those above. 3. <io.TextIOWrapper>.close() -> None  Closes the file.  This is an important step that ensures all the data is written to the file should the program eventually crash. Closing the file also helps prevent data corruption, ensures efficient resource, management and permits other programs to access the data while the Python program continues. 28  Input/Output (IO): Writing files

There are two methods that write text from a file object:

<io.TextIoWrapper>.write(str) -> int Write a single string to a file. Add newline characters “\n” to ensure multiple strings are placed on separate lines. Returns the number of characters written.

<io.TextIoWrapper>.writelines(iterable_str) -> None Write a multiple lines to a file. Expects an iterable of strings, such as [str]. Add newline characters “\n” to each string to place each on separate lines. Include a newline on the last string in the iterable if you expect to write additional information to the file. lines = ["apple\n", "banana\n", "cherry\n"] file.writelines(lines)

Input/Output (IO): Context managers

Context managers are the recommended way to handle IO in Python:

with open(file_path, mode) as file: <indented_code_block>

The context manager ensures that the file is closed automatically for you. During normal execution, the context manager will close the file when evaluation reaches the end of the <indented_code_block>. If there is an exception, Python will close the file as it terminates.

Context managers should be used for both reading and writing tasks.

Input/Output (IO): Best practice

When reading files, the best practice for most applications is a context manager along with file iteration:

with open(file_path, "r") as file: for line in file:

When writing files, the best practice for most applications is a context manager along with .write() and .writelines() methods:

# Either "x" or "w" as the write_mode as appropriate with open(file_path, write_mode) as file:

A filepath specifies the unique location of a file within a computer’s file system hierarchy:

A Filepaths shows the route through directories or folders to reach a particular file. Directory names (folders) are listed in order and separated by delimiters that depend on the operating system. There are two main types of filepaths:  Absolute filepath: Provides the complete location from the root directory to the file, independent of the current working directory.  Relative filepath: Specifies the location relative to the current working directory, not starting from the root.

Filepaths are system specific:

Unix and MacOS: Absolute paths begin with a forward slash (/) Relative paths begins with a directory name Directories are separated by forward slashes (/)

Windows: Absolute paths begin with a device specifier (e.g., c:\) Relative paths begin with a directory name Directories are separated by backslashes (\)

Consider an example in which projects is a top-level directory:

CSV file:  projects -> prop_trading -> data -> stocks.csv Current working directory:  projects -> prop_trading

The absolute and relative paths:

Unix and MacOS  Absolute path: /projects/prop_trading/data/stocks.csv  Relative path: data/stocks.csv Windows  Absolute path: c:\projects\prop_trading\data\stocks.csv  Relative path: data\stocks.csv

We’d like our code to be portable so it functions on different computers and platforms, but using strings for paths is problematic:

Strings are platform-specific, requiring a program adjusts for forward and backslashes automatically. Paths may be corrupted by tab “\t” and newline “\n” characters.  Python can misinterpret a path like “c:\the_directory\new_file.csv” as having a tab character and a newline.  Double backslashes solves this problem, but is error-prone if the programmer forgets.

The solution is to use Python’s pathlib module to create cross-platform, error-free filepaths

Filepaths: The pathlib module and Path class

The pathlib module provides a Path class that solves the platform-specific path problem:

from pathlib import Path p = Path("directory_or_file_name")

When a Path is used in an open statement, Python will adjust the path for platform-specific details.

Filepaths: The pathlib module and Path class

The pathlib module provides two ways to construct paths:

Path / str -> Path Combines a Path with a string (or Path) into a new Path.

p = Path("base_dir") / "sub_dir" / "file.ext" p = Path("base_dir") / Path("sub_dir") / Path("file.ext")

joinpath(*other) -> Path Joins one or more path components.

p = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")

Each Path object or string should contain a single directory level or file.

Filepaths: Common Path methods

The Path class provides several utility functions to inspect the kind of information that exists at a given filepath.

path_obj.exists() –> bool Checks if the path exists in the filesystem. Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext").exists() # True if the file exists at the path, and False otherwise.

path_obj.is_dir() –> bool Checks if the path points to a directory. Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "other_dir").is_dir() # True if the path exists and is a directory, and False otherwise.

path_obj.is_file() –> bool Checks if the path points to a file. Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext").is_dir() # True if the path exists and is a file, and False otherwise.

Filepaths: Common Path attributes

The Path class provides attributes to extract individual components of a filepath.

path_obj.name –> str Returns the final component (file or directory name) of the path. p = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext") p.name # "file.ext"

path_obj.stem Returns the file name without its extension. p.stem # "file"

path_obj.suffix Returns the file extension with the leading dot. p.suffix # ".ext"

path_obj.parent -> Path Returns the directory containing the file or directory. p.parent # PathType('base_dir/sub_dir')

Input/Output (IO) and Filepaths: Best practice  Input/Output (IO) and Filepaths: Best practice

When reading files, the best practice for most applications is using a Path object in a context manager along with file iteration:

from pathlib import Path

file_path = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext") with open(file_path, "r") as file: for line in file:

When writing files, the best practice for most applications is using a Path object in context manager along with .write() and .writelines() methods:

from pathlib import Path

file_path = Path("base_dir").joinpath("sub_dir", "file.ext")

# Either "x" or "w" as the write_mode as appropriate with open(file_path, write_mode) as file:

Comprehensions  Comprehensions

Comprehensions provide a concise way to construct list, dict & set containers.

Comprehensions can be used in place of a for loop to populate standard containers. Comprehensions may make your code easier to read and faster than a corresponding loop.

There are no tuple comprehensions in Python.

Comprehensions: List comprehensions  Comprehensions: List comprehensions

Many list-based loops can be translated into comprehensions

A for loop that creates a list of perfect squares, x2 , where x is from 0 through 100. squares = [] for x in range(100 + 1): squares.append(x**2)

. . . and its corresponding list comprehension. squares = [x**2 for x in range(100 + 1)]

Comprehensions: List comprehension syntax

Lists may be constructed using a comprehension instead of a loop.

[<expression> for <item> in <iterable>]

The basic list comprehension syntax consists of the following: An opening square bracket [ An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the list The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be A closing square bracket ]

Comprehensions: List comprehension syntax

Lists comprehensions may optionally filter elements

[<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>]

The general list comprehension syntax consists of: An opening square bracket [ An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the list The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be An if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True for an element to be included in the resulting list A closing square bracket ]

Comprehensions: List comprehensions and loops

Loops and list comprehensions are two methods to perform a tasks.

A loop. . . lst = [] for <item> in <iterable>: if <condition>: lst.append(<expressio>)

. . . can be translated into a list comprehension. lst = [<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>]

Similarly, a list comprehension can be translated into a loop.

Comprehensions: Set and dictionary comprehensions  Comprehensions: Set comprehensions syntax

The syntax for set comprehensions is:

{<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>}

The set comprehension syntax consists of: An opening curly bracket { An <expression> that uses the <item> item object to produce a result that is placed in the set The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be An (optional) if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True for an element to be included in the resulting set A closing curly bracket }

Comprehensions: Dictionary comprehensions syntax

The syntax for dictionary comprehensions is:

{<key_expr: value_expr> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>}

The dictionary comprehension syntax consists of: An opening curly bracket { A <key_expr: value_expr> expression indicating each key-value pair to place in the dictionary  <key_expr> produces a key object from <item>. The key must be hashable.  <value_expr> produces a value object from <item> The for <item> in <iterable> indicating that each element of the <iterable> should be An (optional) if <condition> that provides a logical <condition> that must evaluate True for an element to be included in the resulting list A closing curly bracket }

Comprehensions: What about tuples?

Python does not support tuple comprehensions.

The following syntax will not produce a tuple:

(<expression> for <item> in <iterable> if <condition>)

Writing parentheses around a comprehension creates a generator expression, not a tuple.

(x**2 for x in range(5)) # <generator object ...>

To get a tuple, you must pass the generator to the tuple constructor:

tuple(x**2 for x in range(5)) # (0, 1, 4, 9, 16)

Generators will not be covered in this course, but it is important to know that ( ... ) creates a generator expression, not a tuple.

Comprehensions: Summary  Comprehensions: Summary

Comprehensions are very flexible!

In this course, we will focus primarily on list and dict comprehensions. We will use standard looping structures when the corresponding comprehension requires complicated nesting or filtering conditions. The official Python Documentation is a good place to learn more about comprehensions:  List comprehensions: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#list-comprehensions  Dict comprehensions: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html#dictionaries  Generator expressions (advanced): https://docs.python.org/3/reference/expressions.html#generator-expressions
